about.title: About Us
about.subtitle: Airport pickup specialists operating from TIV since 2007.
about.seoDesc: We station vehicles in the TIV short-stay car park so every arriving passenger — easyJet, Ryanair, TUI, Wizz Air — walks straight from baggage reclaim to their car.
about.p1: Since 2007, Tivat Car Hire has been connecting travellers with reliable, affordable rental cars from Tivat Airport and Porto Montenegro. What started as a simple idea — making it easier for visitors landing at Tivat to explore this stunning corner of the Adriatic — has grown into one of the area's most trusted car rental services.
about.p2: Tivat Airport is Montenegro's coastal gateway. Most visitors to the Bay of Kotor, Budva Riviera, and Porto Montenegro land here. The airport sits just 4 km from town and 20 minutes from Kotor. Having a car ready at arrivals means you can be exploring the coast within minutes of landing — no transfers, no waiting.
about.p3a: We provide car rental services from
about.p3d: , Budva, and locations across the coast and interior.
about.promise1: Full insurance with zero excess options available
about.promise2: Tivat Airport meet-and-greet — we bring the car to you at arrivals
about.promise3: No hidden fees — the price you see is the price you pay
about.promise4: Cross-border travel permitted to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
about.localText: Based in Tivat, we know the roads, the best routes, and the hidden gems that guidebooks miss. Whether you're driving from
about.localMid2: medieval old town, taking the scenic route through the Bay of Kotor to
about.localEnd: , or heading inland to Durmitor National Park, we can help you plan the perfect road trip.
about.contactText: Have a question about renting a car from Tivat? We're available via phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Search our fleet online and book in minutes — or reach out and we'll help you find the perfect car for your trip.

kotor.title: Tivat to Kotor Car Hire
kotor.subtitle: Through the Vrmac tunnel in 12 minutes — or take the bay road for the views.
kotor.seoDesc: Exit TIV, turn left, enter the Vrmac tunnel and reach Kotor's walls in 12 minutes. Park at River Gate lot (free off-season) or the multi-storey at Tabacina.

tivat-airport.title: Tivat Airport Car Hire
tivat-airport.subtitle: Walk from baggage reclaim to your car — no shuttle bus, no transfer desk.
tivat-airport.seoDesc: TIV is a single-terminal airport. Baggage hall to car park is 40 metres. easyJet, Ryanair, TUI, Wizz Air — we meet every flight, every season.

budva.title: Tivat to Budva Car Hire
budva.subtitle: Exit the airport, take the Vrmac tunnel, hit the riviera in 25 minutes.
budva.seoDesc: From TIV take the Vrmac tunnel shortcut south. Pass the Jaz Beach junction and drop into Budva in 25 minutes. Fuel up at the Petrol station in Radanovici.

perast.title: Tivat to Perast Car Hire
perast.subtitle: Follow the bay road north — 20 minutes of waterfront driving.
perast.seoDesc: Turn left from TIV onto the bay road, pass Muo and Prcanj, and pull into Perast after 20 minutes. Park at the north end — free spots before 9 am in summer.

podgorica.title: Car Hire in Podgorica
podgorica.subtitle: Montenegro's capital — 90 minutes inland from TIV via the Sozina tunnel or the mountain road.
podgorica.seoDesc: Two routes from Tivat: the fast Sozina tunnel toll road (90 min) or the free mountain road over Lovcen (2.5 hr). Both end at Montenegro's compact, walkable capital.

podgorica-airport.title: Podgorica Airport Car Hire
podgorica-airport.subtitle: Montenegro's inland hub — budget carriers, year-round schedule.
podgorica-airport.seoDesc: TGD handles Ryanair, Wizz Air, and year-round Belgrade flights. Collect at arrivals and drive to Tivat's coast in 90 minutes via the Sozina tunnel.

dubrovnik-airport.title: Dubrovnik Airport Car Hire
dubrovnik-airport.subtitle: Land in Croatia, drive to Montenegro — one border, one hour.
dubrovnik-airport.seoDesc: Fly into DBV, collect your car, cross at Debeli Brijeg, and reach Tivat's bay within 60–90 minutes. Green card insurance included, coastal road the whole way.

tivat.title: Car Hire in Tivat
tivat.subtitle: Porto Montenegro marina, Lustica Bay, and the fastest airport pickup on the Adriatic.
tivat.seoDesc: Tivat sits across the road from TIV airport. Porto Montenegro berths 450+ superyachts, Lustica Bay is a 10-minute drive south, and every bay town is under 30 minutes away.

airportPickup.title: 40-Metre Pickup
airportPickup.desc: Your car sits in the TIV short-stay car park. Walk from the baggage belt, cross the road, unlock the car. No shuttle bus exists at this airport — you don't need one.

fullInsurance.title: Full Insurance Included
fullInsurance.desc: Every booking includes CDW and theft protection. Upgrade to zero-excess cover for complete peace of mind on mountain roads and gravel tracks.

support.title: 24/7 Roadside Line
support.desc: Flat tyre on the Kotor serpentines? Locked out at Lustica Bay? Our roadside number connects you to a local mechanic within 30 minutes, any time of day.

noHiddenFees.title: Transparent Pricing
noHiddenFees.desc: VAT, mileage, equipment kit, airport surcharge — everything is folded into the figure on screen. The checkout total is the total, full stop.

freeCancellation.title: Free Cancellation
freeCancellation.desc: Flight moved? Change of plan? Cancel up to 24 hours before pickup at no cost. Useful when airline schedules shift between summer and winter.

crossBorder.title: Cross-Border Ready
crossBorder.desc: Green card paperwork prepared at booking. Drive to Dubrovnik, Mostar, Shkoder, or Belgrade — the documents will be in the glovebox when you collect.

0.text: Ryanair landed at TIV, bags took five minutes, car was in the short-stay lot right across the road. We were at Porto Montenegro ordering lunch before the taxi queue had moved.

1.text: Took the Vrmac tunnel from the airport — 12 minutes later we were parked outside Kotor's walls. The agent had already loaded the Verige ferry schedule into the sat-nav for our Herceg Novi trip the next day.

2.text: Drove a Dacia Duster from TIV down to Lustica Bay, then over to Budva via the back road past Radanovici. Week-long rental, 1,200 km, zero issues. Fuel station right by the airport made drop-off painless.

0.q: What documents do I need at TIV pickup?
0.a: A driving licence with Latin-script details, a passport, and a physical credit card in the main driver's name. If your licence uses only non-Latin characters, bring an International Driving Permit. Electronic licences and photocopies are not accepted.

1.q: Can I drive the rental car to Croatia or Albania?
1.a: Yes. Notify the rental company at booking so they prepare the Green Card insurance certificate. Cross-border travel to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo is permitted — documents will be in the glovebox.

2.q: Is there a minimum age to rent?
2.a: Age and experience requirements depend on the vehicle category. Economy cars typically require 21+, premium vehicles 25+. These details appear during the search before you commit.

3.q: What is included in the rental price?
3.a: CDW and theft protection, VAT, an equipment kit (first-aid box, warning triangle, reflective vest), and a transparent fuel policy. Mileage terms vary by vehicle — some are unlimited, others capped at a daily kilometre allowance shown on the listing.

4.q: How does TIV airport pickup work?
4.a: After you clear baggage reclaim, walk out the terminal exit and cross to the short-stay car park — roughly 40 metres. Our agent meets you there with the keys and paperwork. Total time from bag belt to driving: about 10 minutes.

5.a: Free cancellation up to 7 days before the rental start date for standard vehicles, up to 30 days for premium and convertible categories. A 6% payment processing fee applies to all refunds.

6.a: Deposit amounts vary by vehicle group — some economy cars require no deposit at all. When a deposit applies, it must be on a physical credit card in the main driver's name and is refunded on return.

7.q: Where is the nearest fuel station to TIV?
7.a: Jugopetrol is 800 metres to the right as you exit the airport onto the main road. A second Petrol station sits in Radanovici on the Budva road — useful if you are heading south. Both accept card and cash. Fill up before returning the car to avoid refuelling charges.

tivat-airport.desc: 40 metres from baggage reclaim to the car park.

kotor.desc: Through Vrmac tunnel to the UNESCO walled town.

perast.desc: Baroque waterfront, boat to Our Lady of the Rocks.

budva.name: Budva
budva.desc: Riviera beaches, Jaz, Mogren, Sveti Stefan.

dubrovnik-airport.name: Herceg Novi
dubrovnik-airport.desc: Bay entrance town, near Croatian border.

podgorica.name: Podgorica
podgorica.desc: Capital city via Sozina tunnel or mountain road.

aboutBody.p1: Tivat Car Hire exists for one reason: to get arriving passengers into a rental car as fast as physically possible. TIV is a compact single-terminal airport — no moving walkways, no inter-terminal trains, no shuttle buses. Baggage reclaim sits 40 metres from the short-stay car park. We station vehicles there so you walk from the belt to the driver's seat in minutes, not hours.
aboutBody.h2: Why We Built the Service Around TIV
aboutBody.p2: Every summer, easyJet, Ryanair, TUI, and Wizz Air funnel hundreds of thousands of passengers through a terminal smaller than most supermarkets. That compact layout is an advantage — but only if your car is already on-site. Off-airport depots require transfers that waste the time TIV's design saves. We keep every vehicle within walking distance of arrivals so the small-airport advantage actually works for you.
aboutBody.p3: Clear baggage reclaim, walk through the exit doors, cross the access road to the short-stay car park. Our agent is there with your keys and paperwork. Sign, adjust the mirrors, and drive. Turn right for Budva and the Vrmac tunnel, left along the bay road for Kotor and Perast, or straight ahead for Porto Montenegro and Lustica Bay. A Jugopetrol fuel station sits 800 metres down the road if you need to fill up before heading out.
aboutBody.promise1: CDW and theft protection — zero-excess upgrade available
aboutBody.promise2: Meet-and-greet at the TIV short-stay car park, 40 m from the terminal
aboutBody.promise3: Transparent pricing — the checkout figure is the final figure
aboutBody.promise4: Cross-border Green Card for Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, Kosovo
aboutBody.promise5: Free cancellation up to 7 days before pickup (30 days for premium)
aboutBody.promise6: 24/7 roadside assistance line staffed by local mechanics
aboutBody.p4: We drive these roads daily. We know that the Vrmac tunnel cuts the Kotor run to 12 minutes, that the Verige ferry across the bay mouth saves 45 minutes versus the long road to Herceg Novi, that the Radanovici fuel station is the last stop before the Budva riviera, and that Kotor's Tabacina car park fills by 10 am in July. Ask us anything at pickup — route advice is free and unlimited.
aboutBody.p5: Questions before you fly? WhatsApp, Viber, or phone — we reply within minutes during operating hours. Search the fleet online, lock in a price, and your car will be waiting in the TIV car park when your flight touches down.

kotorBody.h1: Kotor — 12 Minutes Through the Vrmac Tunnel
kotorBody.p1: Exit TIV, turn left, and enter the Vrmac tunnel 3 km down the road. The tunnel cuts straight through the mountain that separates Tivat from Kotor's side of the bay. You emerge at the Kotor approach road — follow signs for Stari Grad (Old Town) and you will reach the city walls in about 12 minutes total from the airport gate.
kotorBody.p2: If you prefer the scenic bay road, stay on the waterfront route through Muo and Prcanj. It adds 8 minutes but follows the water the whole way, with pull-off viewpoints at the narrowest section of the bay. Both routes end at the same roundabout below Kotor's north wall.
kotorBody.stJohnText: Tabacina multi-storey (paid, covered, 5-minute walk to the Sea Gate) fills fast in summer — arrive before 10 am. The River Gate lot along the Skurda stream is free off-season and cheaper than Tabacina year-round. In July and August, a temporary overflow lot operates on the approach road with a shuttle to the walls. Street parking inside the old town does not exist.
kotorBody.cathedralText: The Old Town is car-free and entirely walkable in 20 minutes end to end. The fortress hike (St. John / San Giovanni) starts from the north-east corner — 1,350 steps, allow 45 minutes up and 30 down. The Maritime Museum near the Sea Gate covers Boka Bay's naval history. Most restaurants cluster along the main square and the cathedral plaza.
kotorBody.perastTrip: Continue north along the bay road to Perast (8 minutes from Kotor). Boats to Our Lady of the Rocks island leave from Perast's waterfront — roughly 5 EUR return. Beyond Perast, the road climbs to Risan and then loops back toward Herceg Novi or connects via the Verige ferry shortcut across the bay mouth.
kotorBody.cetinjeTrip: Head inland on the Kotor–Cetinje serpentine road — 25 hairpin bends climbing 1,000 metres above the bay. The viewpoint at the top (Krstac pass) is one of the most photographed spots in Montenegro. Cetinje itself is 45 minutes from Kotor and houses the old royal court and Cetinje Monastery.
kotorBody.royalCourtText: The monastery holds religious relics and sits at the centre of Montenegro's former royal capital. Combined with the serpentine drive, this makes a solid half-day trip from Kotor before returning to Tivat via the tunnel.

budvaBody.h1: Budva — 25 Minutes via the Vrmac Tunnel
budvaBody.p1: From TIV, take the main road south toward Budva. After 3 km you enter the Vrmac tunnel, emerge on the far side at the Tivat–Budva junction, and continue through Radanovici toward the coast. The Radanovici Petrol station is the last fuel stop before the riviera — fill up here if your tank is low. Total drive time from the airport to Budva's Old Town: roughly 25 minutes outside peak traffic.
budvaBody.p2: In July and August, the final stretch through Becici into Budva can slow to a crawl during afternoon hours. If you are heading to Jaz Beach or points north of town, take the Jaz turnoff before the main Budva junction — it bypasses the worst of the summer congestion entirely.
budvaBody.beachesIntro: The Budva Riviera stretches 21 km south from Jaz Beach to Petrovac. Key stops along the coastal road:
budvaBody.mogren: Two cove beaches connected by a tunnel carved through the cliff, walking distance from Budva Old Town. Loungers and a bar operate May–September
budvaBody.jaz: A large sandy bay 3 km before Budva town. Free parking in the upper lot, paid near the water. Hosts summer music events
budvaBody.svetiStefan: The postcard island village 6 km south of Budva. The mainland Milocer beach is public; the island itself is a luxury hotel. Best photographed from the road above
budvaBody.becici: A long, gently sloping sand beach between Budva and Sveti Stefan. Family-friendly, lifeguarded in season, ample paid parking
budvaBody.oldTownText: The walled peninsula is compact enough to walk in 15 minutes. Enter through the main gate on the landward side, loop through the cobblestone streets, and exit at the Citadela for a view south toward Sveti Stefan. Restaurants inside the walls charge a premium — locals eat at the places one block inland from the gate. Evening parking along the quay is easier to find than daytime spots.
budvaBody.svetiStefanTrip: Drive 6 km south on the coastal road to the Sveti Stefan viewpoint. The pull-off is on the right, marked by a stone wall. From there, walk down to Milocer beach (public, free entry). The Queen's Beach next door requires a hotel pass.
budvaBody.kotorTrip: Head back through the Vrmac tunnel to Kotor — 25 minutes in reverse. Combine with Perast and the bay road for a full-day loop: Budva > Kotor > Perast > Verige ferry > Herceg Novi > back via the coastal road.
budvaBody.lovcenTrip: The inland mountain road to Lovcen National Park starts near Cetinje. Allow 90 minutes from Budva. The Njegos Mausoleum sits at 1,657 metres — carry a jacket even in summer. The winding road has no guardrails in places, so daylight driving is strongly advised.
budvaBody.gettingAroundText: Budva has two main paid car parks: the underground lot near the Old Town walls and the open lot behind the bus station. Both fill by mid-morning in summer. Free street parking exists along the Becici road but requires a 10-minute walk. The nearest fuel station to Budva centre is on the main road at the Becici exit; the Radanovici station is more convenient if you are heading back to TIV.

perastBody.h1: Perast — 20 Minutes Along the Bay Road
perastBody.p1: From TIV, turn left out of the airport and follow the bay road north through Lepetane, Muo, and Prcanj. The road hugs the waterline with the bay on your right and limestone cliffs on your left. After roughly 20 minutes, you arrive at Perast's single main street — a one-lane waterfront promenade lined with stone palaces.
perastBody.p2: Parking in Perast is limited to a small lot at the north end of the village and roadside spaces along the approach. In summer, arrive before 9 am or after 5 pm to find a spot without circling. There are no car parks inside the village itself — the street is too narrow.
perastBody.stGeorgeText: The natural island sits 200 metres offshore, recognisable by its tall dark cypresses and the 12th-century Benedictine monastery behind them. The island is closed to visitors, but boats from Perast's jetty circle it closely for photographs. Views are best in early morning when the bay is flat.
perastBody.ladyOfRocksTitle: Our Lady of the Rocks (Gospa od Skrpjela)
perastBody.ladyOfRocksText: A man-made island built by locals throwing rocks and sinking old boats over centuries. The tradition continues every July 22nd during the Fasinada festival, when residents fill boats with stones and motor out to reinforce the platform. Taxi boats from Perast's waterfront run every 15 minutes in season — roughly 5 EUR return. The small church inside holds a collection of votive paintings by Boka sailors.
perastBody.historicText: The village has no traffic — just one paved promenade along the water. Walk south to north in 10 minutes, passing stone palaces, the tall bell tower of St. Nicholas church, and several waterfront cafes with bay views. The Perast Museum occupies the Buiovic Palace at the south end and documents the town's maritime history, including its role training sailors for the Venetian fleet.
perastBody.monasteryText: Continue north past Perast toward Risan for 2 km and you reach the Banja Monastery on the right side of the road. The monastery holds religious artefacts from Russian, Greek, and local Boka dynasties. It is usually open mornings only — check at the Perast tourist office on the waterfront.

tivatBody.h1: Tivat — Porto Montenegro and the Bay Gateway
tivatBody.p1: Tivat sits directly across the road from TIV airport. Walk out of the terminal, cross the access road, and you are already in town. That proximity makes Tivat the most convenient base for anyone renting a car at the airport — you can park, check in to your accommodation, and head out exploring without fighting any traffic.
tivatBody.p2: The town itself is compact: a 2 km waterfront promenade connects the ferry pier at one end to Porto Montenegro marina at the other. Most restaurants, shops, and cafes line this strip. The population sits around 15,000 year-round but swells considerably in summer when charter flights from across Europe fill the terminal daily.
tivatBody.beachesText: Tivat's 17 beaches span 3 km of coastline along the bay. The water is calmer here than on the open Adriatic coast south of Budva. Top picks:
tivatBody.beach1: Plavi Horizonri (Blue Horizons) — white sand, shallow entry, ideal for families. Located 2 km south of the marina, signposted from the main road
tivatBody.beach2: Lustica Bay resort beaches — 10 minutes south of the airport on the Lustica peninsula road. The development includes the Chedi hotel, a golf course, and managed beach clubs with lounger rental
tivatBody.portoText: The marina occupies the site of the former Yugoslav naval shipyard and now berths 450+ vessels including superyachts up to 250 metres. The waterfront village has boutiques, restaurants, the Regent Porto Montenegro hotel, and the upcoming One&Only resort. A pool club, yacht club, and regular events (regattas, food festivals) operate through the summer season. It is walkable from TIV airport in about 15 minutes.
tivatBody.monasteryText: A 10-minute drive south of Porto Montenegro brings you to the Prevlaka peninsula, where the ruins of the 10th-century St. Michael Archangel Monastery sit among olive trees. Archaeological work continues on-site. The peninsula also has a quiet pebble beach facing the open bay.
tivatBody.churchText: A hillside village 3 km above Tivat, reached by a narrow switchback road. Stone houses, an old church, and a terrace restaurant with a panoramic view over the entire bay. It is the kind of place that rewards having your own car — no bus service runs here.
tivatBody.museumText: For maritime history, drive 12 minutes through the Vrmac tunnel to the Kotor Maritime Museum inside the Old Town walls. Combined with a walk around the fortifications, this makes a comfortable half-day trip from Tivat.

tivatAirportBody.p1: Tivat Airport (IATA: TIV) is a single-terminal facility on the bay shore, handling over a million passengers during peak season. The terminal layout works in your favour: domestic and international arrivals share one baggage hall, and the exit doors lead directly to the short-stay car park across a single access road — roughly 40 metres door to door. No shuttle buses operate at TIV because none are needed.
tivatAirportBody.p2: Summer schedules bring easyJet (London Gatwick, Manchester, Edinburgh, Bristol, Belfast), Ryanair (London Stansted, Milan Bergamo, Berlin), TUI (UK charter programme), and Wizz Air (Vienna, Budapest, Dortmund, Memmingen). Winter service drops to daily Belgrade flights and occasional charters, so availability and pricing shift between seasons. Book early for July–August arrivals when demand peaks.
tivatAirportBody.dist1: Kotor: 12 minutes via Vrmac tunnel, 20 minutes via bay road
tivatAirportBody.dist2: Budva: 25 minutes via Vrmac tunnel and Radanovici road
tivatAirportBody.dist3: Herceg Novi: 35 minutes via Verige ferry, 55 minutes via bay road
tivatAirportBody.dist4: Perast: 20 minutes along the bay road north
tivatAirportBody.facilitiesText: The terminal has one departures hall (upstairs) and one arrivals hall (ground floor). Inside you will find a cafe, a small duty-free shop, ATMs, and a tourist information desk. Outside the arrivals exit, taxis queue on the left, the short-stay car park is directly ahead, and the main road runs parallel on the right. A Jugopetrol fuel station is 800 metres to the right along the main road — useful for topping up before your return drop-off.
tivatAirportBody.whyText: TIV has no inter-terminal transit, no car rental village, no shuttle bus loop. That means your car is closer to the baggage belt than it would be at almost any major European airport. Walk out, cross the road, find your vehicle. The total time from wheels-down to driving off averages 10 minutes for pre-booked customers — faster than clearing the taxi rank.
tivatAirportBody.meetText: Our agent meets you at the short-stay car park with keys and paperwork. We monitor flight arrivals in real time, so delays do not affect your pickup. If your flight lands at 2 am, we will be there at 2 am.

podgoricaBody.p1: Two routes connect Tivat to Montenegro's capital. The fast option is the Sozina tunnel toll road: drive south toward Budva, turn inland at Virpazar, and enter the 4.2 km tunnel that punches through to the Podgorica plain. Total time: roughly 90 minutes, toll approximately 3.50 EUR. The free alternative is the mountain road over Lovcen — dramatically scenic but allow 2.5 hours with switchbacks and no overtaking.
podgoricaBody.p2: Podgorica sits on the confluence of six rivers (Moraca, Ribnica, Zeta, Sitnica, Mareza, Cijevna) and has a population of roughly 170,000. The architecture reflects every era of occupation: Ottoman clock towers, Austro-Hungarian facades, Yugoslav-era concrete blocks, and new glass office buildings along Bulevar Svetog Petra Cetinjskog.
podgoricaBody.p3: The city functions as Montenegro's commercial and administrative hub. It lacks the tourist density of the coast, which means lower prices, easier parking, and restaurant tables without reservations. For anyone with a rental car, it works well as a day trip or as an overnight stop en route to the northern mountains.
podgoricaBody.seeText: The Millennium Bridge is the most recognisable landmark — a cable-stayed span over the Moraca. The old Turkish Quarter (Stara Varos) sits on the south bank with narrow streets, a clock tower, and a few traditional restaurants. Delta City shopping centre on the northern ring road has international brands, a cinema, and underground parking. For local produce, the Green Market operates daily near the central square.
podgoricaBody.skadarText: Drive 30 minutes south on the Virpazar road to reach the largest lake in the Balkans, straddling the Montenegro–Albania border. Boat tours launch from Virpazar village. The lake is a national park, home to pelican colonies and 260+ bird species. Bring binoculars and mosquito repellent in summer.
podgoricaBody.danilovgradText: A quiet river town 25 minutes west of Podgorica on the E762. Riverside beaches along the Zeta River, a couple of fish restaurants, and the Zdrebaonik monastery 5 km north. Low-key, good for a half-day escape from the capital.
podgoricaBody.zabljak: A mountain town at 1,450 metres in the Durmitor range, 170 km north of Podgorica via the E65. In winter: skiing at Savin Kuk. In summer: Tara River canyon rafting, Black Lake hiking, and the Durmitor ring road. The drive takes 2.5–3 hours depending on conditions — fill up in Podgorica, as fuel stations are sparse north of Kolasin.

podgoricaAirportBody.h1: Collecting Your Car at Podgorica Airport (TGD)
podgoricaAirportBody.p1: TGD handles an increasing share of Montenegro's air traffic, particularly budget carriers. Ryanair and Wizz Air operate seasonal routes from across Europe, while Air Serbia maintains a daily Belgrade connection year-round. If your flight lands at TGD instead of TIV, collecting a car here and driving to the coast is straightforward — the Sozina tunnel puts you on the bay in 90 minutes.
podgoricaAirportBody.p2: The airport opened a new terminal building in 2006; the old terminal now functions as a small aviation museum. The IATA code TGD references the city's former name, Titograd. The airport sits 12 km south of the city centre, connected by the main highway.
podgoricaAirportBody.distIntro: TGD is 12 km south of Podgorica centre, well-connected to the national road network:
podgoricaAirportBody.dist2: Tivat / Bay of Kotor: 90 minutes via Sozina tunnel (toll ~3.50 EUR)
podgoricaAirportBody.dist3: Budva: 75 minutes via Sozina tunnel and Radanovici road
podgoricaAirportBody.dist4: Kolasin (ski resort): 80 km north, roughly 75 minutes
podgoricaAirportBody.flightsText: Year-round: Air Serbia (Belgrade daily), occasional Ryanair and Wizz Air winter schedules. Summer peak: Ryanair (London Stansted, Milan Bergamo, Brussels Charleroi), Wizz Air (Vienna, Budapest, Dortmund, Memmingen), TUI charters. Seasonal routes to Paris, Rome, Frankfurt, and Vienna also operate. Check airline schedules — frequency drops sharply from October to April.
podgoricaAirportBody.facilitiesText: TGD is a single-terminal airport with eight departure gates, two arrival gates, and roughly 5,500 m² of floor space. The car park holds 600+ vehicles with 25,000 m² of surface area — parking is never a problem here. Inside: a cafe, duty-free shop, ATM, and bureau de change.
podgoricaAirportBody.awardText: Despite its modest size, TGD won the ACI Europe award for best airport under one million passengers — a recognition of its clean facilities and efficient layout.
podgoricaAirportBody.rentalText: Walk out of the arrivals hall and our agent meets you at the car park with your keys and documents. No shuttle bus, no off-site depot. Your car is right there. If you are driving to the coast, head south and follow signs for Virpazar / Sozina — the tunnel entrance is about 40 minutes from TGD. Fill up in Podgorica before entering the tunnel; fuel stations on the mountain route are infrequent.

dubrovnikAirportBody.h1: Collecting Your Car at Dubrovnik Airport (DBV)
dubrovnikAirportBody.p1: Dubrovnik Airport is the busiest arrival point for tourists heading to southern Montenegro. The airport handles a wider range of airlines than TIV or TGD — British Airways, easyJet, Norwegian, Eurowings, Vueling, and many charter operators fly here. If your preferred carrier does not serve Tivat directly, landing at DBV and driving south is the standard workaround.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p2: The drive from DBV to the Montenegro border takes about 30 minutes on the coastal E65. After the Debeli Brijeg checkpoint, continue along the bay to Herceg Novi (15 min from the border), the Verige ferry crossing, and onward to Tivat (60–90 min total depending on route). Ensure your rental company has included a Green Card for Montenegro — without it, border guards will turn you back.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p3: One practical note: Croatian motorway tolls apply if you take the A1 from the airport toward the border. The toll is modest (under 5 EUR) and can be paid by card or cash at the booth. The alternative coastal road avoids the toll but adds 10 minutes.
dubrovnikAirportBody.borderText: The Debeli Brijeg / Konfin checkpoint is the only coastal crossing between Croatia and Montenegro. In summer (July–August), expect 30–90 minute queues during mid-day. Crossing before 8 am or after 8 pm cuts wait times dramatically. Have your passport, rental contract, and Green Card ready in hand — not buried in a suitcase. Croatian exit and Montenegrin entry are processed at the same stop.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText1: The terminal sits 20 km south-east of Dubrovnik Old Town on the E65 coastal highway. Short-stay parking is limited and expensive in season — if you are collecting a rental car, pick it up from the airport lot and drive directly rather than parking first. A bureau de change, ATMs, and a duty-free shop are inside the terminal.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText2: Rental car desks are in the arrivals hall. Collect your paperwork inside, then walk to the car park outside. Some agencies use off-site lots with shuttle transfers — confirm in advance whether your car is on-site or requires a bus ride.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText1: Exit DBV and turn right (south-east) onto the E65. Follow the coastal road through Cavtat and Gruda toward the border. The road is well-maintained, two lanes, with sea views for much of the route. After the border, the road drops into Igalo and Herceg Novi before following the bay to Tivat.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText2: Two route options from Herceg Novi to Tivat: the long bay road through Risan and Perast (scenic, 55 minutes) or the Verige ferry at Kamenari–Lepetane (runs every 15 minutes, 5 minutes crossing, saves 30+ minutes). The ferry costs about 5 EUR per car and accepts cash only. If arriving late at night, check the ferry timetable — frequency drops after 11 pm.

montenegroBody.h1: Driving Montenegro from Tivat — A Practical Road Guide
montenegroBody.p1: Montenegro is small on the map but slow on the road. Mountain switchbacks, single-lane tunnels, and winding bay roads mean a 50 km drive can take an hour. That is not a complaint — the scenery is the reason most people rent a car. But plan your daily distances realistically. Two destinations per day is comfortable. Three is rushed.
montenegroBody.p2: TIV airport sits roughly halfway along the Bay of Kotor, which means you are already in the middle of the action when you collect your car. Kotor is north, Budva is south, Porto Montenegro is across the road, and inland Montenegro — Cetinje, Podgorica, Durmitor — branches off via tunnels and mountain passes.
montenegroBody.startText: Exit the TIV car park and you hit the main road immediately. Turn left (north) for Kotor via the Vrmac tunnel (12 min) or the bay road (20 min). Turn right (south) for Budva via Radanovici (25 min). Straight ahead across the roundabout reaches Porto Montenegro marina in 3 minutes. The Jugopetrol fuel station is 800 metres to the right — fill up before heading out, especially if driving to Podgorica where mountain-route fuel stops are scarce.
montenegroBody.timesText1: Kotor: 12 min (tunnel) / 20 min (bay road). Perast: 20 min. Budva: 25 min. Herceg Novi: 35 min (Verige ferry) / 55 min (bay road). Sveti Stefan: 30 min. Cetinje: 60 min. Podgorica: 90 min (Sozina tunnel) / 2.5 hr (mountain road). Durmitor / Zabljak: 4 hr.
montenegroBody.timesText2: These times assume dry conditions and light traffic. In July–August, add 15–20 minutes to any route passing through Budva or the Kotor approach road. The Verige ferry at Kamenari–Lepetane runs every 15 minutes, costs about 5 EUR, and saves 30+ minutes versus driving around the full bay to Herceg Novi.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Police checkpoints appear randomly on main roads. They are routine and nothing to worry about if you carry:
montenegroBody.rule1: Driving licence (international permit accepted alongside national licence)
montenegroBody.rule2: Rental contract — original, not a photocopy or screenshot
montenegroBody.rule3: Insurance documents (should be in the glovebox from pickup)
montenegroBody.rule4: Green Card if crossing any border (about 15 EUR for 15 days, arranged at booking)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seat belts mandatory for all passengers, front and rear
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Zero tolerance for mobile phone use while driving — hands-free only
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Zero alcohol tolerance — the legal limit is 0.00%
montenegroBody.keyRule4: Speed limits: 50 km/h in towns, 80 km/h on open roads, lower on mountain sections
montenegroBody.conditionsText: Main coastal roads (E65/E80) are well-surfaced and marked. Mountain roads vary — some have no guardrails, limited lighting, and single-lane sections with blind corners. Fuel stations are plentiful along the coast (Jugopetrol, Petrol, Ina) but sparse once you head inland past Cetinje. Fill up before mountain routes. Diesel is cheaper than petrol; LPG is available at larger stations.
montenegroBody.e65Text: Follows the entire Bay of Kotor shoreline from Herceg Novi through Risan, Perast, and Kotor, then south past Tivat to Budva. This is the scenic backbone of coastal Montenegro. Sections between Kotor and Perast are narrow with blind corners — use your horn on tight bends. The Verige ferry at Kamenari shortcutting across the bay mouth is part of this route network.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Connects the coast to Podgorica via the 4.2 km Sozina toll tunnel. The toll is approximately 3.50 EUR (cash or card). This is the fastest route to the capital and avoids the dramatic but slow mountain road over Lovcen. North of Podgorica, the E65 continues to Kolasin and eventually Zabljak in the Durmitor mountains.
montenegroBody.borderText: Cross-border travel is standard with a Green Card in the glovebox. The Croatia crossing at Debeli Brijeg is busiest (July–August mid-day queues of 1–2 hours). Albania via Sukobin/Muriqan near Ulcinj is quicker. Bosnia via Scepan Polje or Vilusi is rarely congested. Always have passport, rental contract, and Green Card accessible — not in the boot.
montenegroBody.nationText: Summer (June–September): peak traffic, full car parks, higher rental prices, daily flights from across Europe. Book vehicles and accommodation early. Winter (October–May): quieter roads, lower prices, reduced ferry frequency, mountain passes occasionally closed by snow. The Vrmac tunnel and Sozina tunnel operate year-round regardless of weather.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Taking Your Rental Car Across Borders
borderCrossingBody.p1: Cross-border travel from Montenegro is permitted to Croatia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. The process is straightforward: notify your rental company at booking, they prepare the Green Card insurance certificate, and you collect it with the car at TIV. The Green Card and your rental contract go in the glovebox — border guards will ask for both.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card is an international motor insurance certificate proving your vehicle is covered in the country you are entering. Without it, border guards will refuse entry and send you back. It is the single most important document for cross-border driving.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard1: Cost: approximately 15 EUR for 15 days coverage
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Arranged by the rental company at booking — just confirm which countries you plan to visit
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Covers: Croatia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Albania, Serbia, Kosovo (confirm Kosovo separately — some policies exclude it)
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Check the Green Card lists every country on your itinerary BEFORE you leave the airport. Turning back from a border 2 hours into your trip wastes a day.
borderCrossingBody.docsTitle: Documents to Have in Hand at Every Checkpoint
borderCrossingBody.doc1: Valid passport or EU national ID card
borderCrossingBody.doc2: Driving licence (international driving permit if licence is non-Latin script only)
borderCrossingBody.doc3: Rental contract — the original paper copy, not a phone screenshot
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration document (stays in the glovebox from pickup, do not remove it)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The only coastal crossing. Located on the E65 between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik. Croatian and Montenegrin passport control operate at the same stop — you do not leave the car. In July–August, mid-day queues reach 1–2 hours. Early morning (before 8 am) or late evening (after 8 pm) cuts the wait to under 15 minutes.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: Weekday mornings are fastest. Sunday afternoons returning from Dubrovnik are the worst. Have documents in your lap, not in the boot or a suitcase.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: The Kamenari–Lepetane ferry across the Bay of Kotor mouth saves 45 minutes versus the long bay road to Herceg Novi. Take the ferry first, then drive to the border from Herceg Novi — total TIV-to-Dubrovnik time roughly 90 minutes plus border queue.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: Two crossings: Sukobin/Muriqan on the coast near Ulcinj (faster, more tourist-friendly) and Hani i Hotit inland near Skadar Lake. The coastal crossing typically has shorter queues. Albanian road quality drops significantly after the border — drive cautiously, especially on rural routes south of Shkoder.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Fill up before crossing — Albanian fuel stations are less frequent and card payment is not always available. Carry cash (EUR accepted widely in northern Albania).
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: Main crossings: Scepan Polje near Foca (north, quiet, scenic route along the Tara canyon) and Vilusi from Niksic (west, connects to Trebinje and eventually Mostar). Both are rarely congested. Bosnian roads are well-maintained on main routes but deteriorate on rural sections.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: Cross at Dobrakovo on the E65 north of Bijelo Polje. This connects to the Serbian motorway network toward Belgrade (roughly 4 hours from the border). The crossing itself is efficient — typically under 15 minutes outside peak holiday weekends.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The Kula crossing near Rozaje connects to Peja/Pec in western Kosovo. Confirm with your rental company that Kosovo is listed on your Green Card — a small number of policies exclude it. The road on the Kosovo side is reasonable but narrow in sections.
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Arrange documents in order (passport, licence, rental contract, Green Card) before joining the queue — fumbling at the window adds minutes per car
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Switch off the engine while waiting — border guards at Debeli Brijeg will ask you to if you do not
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Avoid Sunday afternoons at the Croatia crossing — returning Dubrovnik day-trippers create the longest queues of the week
borderCrossingBody.tip4: Inland crossings (Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) rarely have significant waits, even in peak summer
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Carry cash in EUR — useful for tolls and small purchases on the other side before you find an ATM
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Check mobile data roaming — your Montenegro SIM may not cover the country you are entering without an add-on
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Traffic Laws That Change at the Border
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Each neighbouring country has slightly different rules. Key things to note:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: headlights on at all times. Motorway tolls apply (cash or card at booths). Zero alcohol tolerance
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: lower speed limits on rural roads. Road quality drops sharply outside main cities. Carry cash for fuel
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres mandatory November–April. Carry first-aid kit and warning triangle (should already be in the car)
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: zero alcohol tolerance. Motorway vignette required (buy at border petrol stations or online)
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: drive on the right. International driving permit recommended. Road signs may be in Albanian and Serbian
borderCrossingBody.route1: TIV to Dubrovnik: 90 min via Verige ferry + Herceg Novi + Debeli Brijeg crossing. Best as a day trip — leave by 7 am, return by 8 pm
borderCrossingBody.route2: TIV to Mostar (Bosnia): 3.5 hr via Trebinje. The Stari Most bridge, Ottoman quarter, and river swimming. Overnight recommended
borderCrossingBody.route3: TIV to Shkoder (Albania): 3 hr via Ulcinj and the Sukobin coastal crossing. Rozafa Castle, Skadar lakeside, cheap restaurants
borderCrossingBody.route4: TIV to Belgrade (Serbia): 6–7 hr via E65 north and Dobrakovo crossing. Long drive — break overnight in Kolasin or Uzice
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Need a rental car with cross-border documents ready from day one? Search our fleet, select your destination countries at booking, and the Green Card will be in the glovebox when you collect at TIV.
